one_line_takeaway: PF-PPO improves RLHF policy quality on code and math by keeping PPO training samples only in the high/low-reward regions where a noisy reward model is trustworthy, with rank-based best-random/best-worst filtering beating strong PPO and best-of-N baselines while a simple R^2 metric predicts which filter to use.
key_finding: PF-PPO with best-random (BR) and best-worst (BW) filtering gives the best code-generation pass@1, reaching 82.9 (HumanEval) / 75.9 (MBPP) / 33.0 (LeetCode) for BR and 82.4 / 76.2 / 30.4 for BW, versus the SFT start of 74.2 / 70.8 / 15.2 and the strongest baselines BOND (80.8 / 75.2 / 30.0) and PPO-M (80.2 / 75.0 / 29.8). A crucial negative result is that PF-PPO (BoN) fails (75.8 / 71.7 / 16.8, below plain PPO-M), showing that unlike SFT-based BoN distillation, an RL algorithm still needs the low-reward 'bad' samples for its contrastive/advantage signal. R^2 correlates with downstream pass@1 (e.g. BW R^2=0.952 vs BoN R^2=0.454 on the SFT policy), validating it as a pre-training selector. Gains are largest on the harder LeetCode benchmark, where the reward model is least reliable. In math, PF-PPO consistently beats PPO-S across all three reward types, and notably an Oracle reward underperforms a learned reward model (PF-PPO+ORM 86.2 vs PF-PPO+Oracle 83.8 on Ape210K), echoing Qwen-Math/DeepSeek-Math.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Policy Filtration for Proximal Policy Optimization (PF-PPO), a modification to the standard PPO stage of RLHF that filters the N sampled responses per prompt before using them to update the policy, keeping only samples in the regions where the reward model is empirically more reliable. The authors' own motivating observation is that a Bradley-Terry reward model is more reliable when it assigns high or low rewards than when it assigns moderate rewards (Figure 1, replicated across MBPP, LeetCode, Ape210K, CMATH in the appendix). Their concrete contributions are: (1) a rank-based filtered-policy family with best-of-N (BoN), best-random (BR), and best-worst (BW) weight vectors that only changes PPO's sampling step (Algorithm 2); (2) using the coefficient of determination R^2 between reward-model scores and ground-truth actual scores as a cheap, pre-training selector for which filtering strategy to use; and (3) a self-constructed LeetCode Contest benchmark (160 competition-level problems, 100 test cases each, from July 2022-Jan 2024). This is the paper's OWN work, distinct from cited best-of-N / reject-sampling-distillation methods (RAFT, BOND, RRHF, PRO) which the Related Work section contrasts against because those use supervised losses rather than an RL algorithm.
threat_model: None. This is a reinforcement-learning / LLM-alignment methods paper about improving policy quality under a noisy (inaccurate) reward model. There is no adversary, no attack, and no security threat model. 'Noise' here means the intrinsic inaccuracy of a learned reward model on hard-to-score responses, not an adversarial manipulation. The paper mentions reward hacking / reward over-optimization (citing Gao et al. 2023, Skalse et al. 2022) as a motivation, which is alignment-adjacent, but it never frames or evaluates a security threat, so no threat model is imputed.
limitations: The authors themselves note R^2 is an imperfect strategy selector and that the best filtering strategy (BR vs BW vs threshold/reweighting variants) varies across tasks, reward models, and base models, so their recommended BR/BW is setting-specific rather than universal. The reliability-region observation is empirical/heuristic, not theoretically derived. Reward-model 'actual scores' rely on executable/verifiable domains (code test cases, math answer checking), so the reliability measurement and the R^2 selector may not transfer to open-ended tasks lacking a ground-truth verifier (the paper only conjectures it extends to helpfulness/harmlessness). The LeetCode benchmark is self-constructed, and the wider-task and math results use proprietary backbones (Doubao-25k) and reward models, limiting reproducibility. Gains on some wider tasks are within noise (e.g. Code BO1 -0.2%, STEM -0.1%).

datasets_used: LeetCode Contest (self-constructed, 160 problems, 100 test cases each, July 2022-Jan 2024)
Ape210K (200k train split, 5k eval split; elementary math)
CMATH (1.7k Chinese elementary math word problems)
SFT/reward data: Magicoder-OSS-instruct (75k) + evol-codealpaca-v1 (55k) = 130k; 7k reward-model prompt pairs; 3k PPO query prompts
Wider-task multi-task suite on Doubao-25k backbone (logic, math, code, STEM, complex, instruction-following, knowledge, language understanding)
quantitative_results: PF-PPO (BR) code pass@1: HumanEval 82.9, MBPP 75.9, LeetCode 33.0 (best overall)
PF-PPO (BW) code pass@1: HumanEval 82.4, MBPP 76.2, LeetCode 30.4
PF-PPO (BoN) code pass@1: HumanEval 75.8, MBPP 71.7, LeetCode 16.8 (fails, below PPO-M)
R^2 on SFT policy (Table 3): PPO 0.782, PPO-M 0.886, PF-PPO BoN 0.454, BR 0.841, BW 0.952
Math Ape210K/CMATH: PF-PPO+ORM 86.2/95.1 vs PPO-S+ORM 84.1/92.3; PF-PPO+Oracle 83.8/91.2 vs PPO-S+Oracle 82.1/90.8; PF-PPO+CRM 84.3/94.2 vs PPO-S+CRM 83.9/93.1

Wider tasks (PF-PPO BO5 accuracy, delta vs PPO-S): Logic 63.8 (+2.8), Math 79.9 (+2.3), Language Understanding 68.4 (+3.8), Instruction Following 59.8 (+1.8); Code 67.4 (+0.1) and STEM 63.1 (+0.1) within noise
Abstract-stated SOTA gains for 7B models: HumanEval +7.9%, MBPP +0.7%, LeetCode Contest +10.0%
baselines_compared: Alternative filters: PPO-top, PPO-top-random, PPO-top-bottom, PPO-pow-k (reward reweighting)
